content:
First note that any “block” of 1’s of length $k\ge4$ has value  
1111…1₍ₖ₎=\frac{10^k-1}9\ge1111>1000,  
so all blocks must have length 1,2 or 3.  Say we use  
  $x$ blocks of “1” (value 1),  
  $y$ blocks of “11” (value 11),  
  $z$ blocks of “111” (value 111).  
Then the total is  
  $x+11y+111z=1000$,  
and the total length is  
  $n=x+2y+3z$.  
From the first equation $x=1000-11y-111z$, so  
  $n=(1000-11y-111z)+2y+3z=1000-9(y+12z)\,. $  
Let $t=y+12z\ge0$.  Then $n=1000-9t$, and the inequality $x\ge0$ becomes  
  $11y+111z\le1000\implies y\le\lfloor(1000-111z)/11\rfloor$,  
which forces  
  $t=y+12z\le\Big\lfloor\frac{1000+21z}{11}\Big\rfloor\,. $  
One checks (by letting $z=0,1,\dots,9$) that the admissible $t$ form  
  $\{0,1,2,\dots,106\}\cup\{108\}$,  
i.e.\ $107$ consecutive values $0\le t\le106$ plus the extra $t=108$.  
Hence there are $108$ possible values of $t$, and so $108$ possible $n=1000-9t$.  
Therefore  
  $a=108\,,\quad$sum of digits of $a=1+0+8=9\,. $  
Answer: \boxed{9}.
